perturbation.original_text: A *donation of $0.05 per Unit sold of Licensed Products within the Contract Territory payable to the **NFL Alumni Northern California Chapter. Donated amounts will be allocated and dispersed to the Northern California Chapter beginning on the first full quarter [three (3) month period] of the Agreement and continue on a quarterly basis thereafter for the term of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: A donation of approximately $0.05 per Unit sold of Licensed Products within the Contract Territory may be payable to the NFL Alumni Northern California Chapter. Donated amounts may be allocated and dispersed to the Northern California Chapter when feasible.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "donation of $0.05 per Unit" to "donation of approximately $0.05 per Unit" introduces ambiguity about the exact amount to be donated. Additionally, changing "will be allocated and dispersed" to "may be allocated and dispersed when feasible" makes the donation discretionary rather than mandatory. This weakens the obligation to donate, creating uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Corporations Code Section 5265 requires adherence to agreements regarding donations to nonprofit organizations. By making the donation amount and disbursement discretionary ('approximately' and 'when feasible'), the modified text creates a situation where the company could argue it is not strictly bound to donate $0.05 per unit or to allocate and disperse the funds quarterly, potentially violating the spirit and intent of the agreement to donate, which is problematic for a charitable corporation relying on those funds.

perturbation.justification: The original contract language specifies a 'donation of $0.05 per Unit' which is a clear and defined obligation. The changed text uses 'approximately $0.05 per Unit,' introducing ambiguity. Additionally, 'will be allocated and dispersed' is changed to 'may be allocated and dispersed when feasible,' making the donation discretionary. The Law Explanation states that this change could potentially violate the spirit and intent of the agreement, as it reduces the binding nature of the donation. The Perturbation Explanation confirms that the modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to donate. The change moves from a definite commitment to a discretionary one, which introduces a legal contradiction as defined in the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: The Company will provide to the NFLA-NC upon request the most recent quarterly sales report of the Company's Licensed Products.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will endeavor to provide to the NFLA-NC upon request a sales report of the Company's Licensed Products when available.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will provide...the most recent quarterly sales report' to 'will endeavor to provide...a sales report...when available' weakens the obligation. 'Endeavor to provide' implies an attempt but not a guarantee, and 'when available' introduces uncertainty about the frequency and timeliness of the report, contradicting the implicit quarterly reporting schedule.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Corporations Code Section 6321 pertains to the right of members to inspect and copy records. While this agreement isn't directly about membership, the implied transparency about sales directly affects the donation amount that the NFLA-NC, a charitable organization, is entitled to. By making the provision of sales reports discretionary ('endeavor to provide' and 'when available'), the modified text reduces the transparency and accountability the NFLA-NC has over the owed donation amounts, potentially creating a conflict of interest and hindering the charitable organization's oversight capabilities.

That report shall contain in appropriate detail the following: (1) The assets and liabilities, including the trust funds, of the corporation as of the end of the fiscal year. (2) The principal changes in assets and liabilities, including trust funds, during the fiscal year. (3) The revenu
perturbation.justification: The change from 'will provide...the most recent quarterly sales report' to 'will endeavor to provide...a sales report...when available' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation, potentially contradicting California Corporations Code Section 6321. The original text implies a mandatory and timely report, while the changed text introduces discretion and uncertainty. Although the snippet describes that a report must be sent, weakening the language means that the NFLA-NC may not have sufficient ability to inspect records because the reports may not be provided or not be provided in a timely matter.

perturbation.original_text: Parties agree to the addition of Gridiron CBD H2O Probiotic™ Water to "Licensed Products" as follows: SECTION ONE. DEFINITIONS As used in this Agreement, the following terms shall be defined as follows: F. "Licensed Products" shall mean BlackMP Living Water, BlackMP Concentrate, Zezel Probiotic Water, Zayin Sports Water, Gridiron CBD H2O Probiotics™ Water, Gridiron MVP™ and Gridiron MVP™ Concentrate using the Pro Football Legends Logo on the Licensed Products' affixed labels, hang-tags or packaging.
perturbation.changed_text: Parties agree to consider the addition of Gridiron CBD H2O Probiotic™ Water as a "Licensed Product." SECTION ONE. DEFINITIONS As used in this Agreement, the following terms may be defined as follows: F. "Licensed Products" might mean BlackMP Living Water, BlackMP Concentrate, Zezel Probiotic Water, Zayin Sports Water, Gridiron CBD H2O Probiotics™ Water, Gridiron MVP™ and Gridiron MVP™ Concentrate using the Pro Football Legends Logo on applicable Products' affixed labels, hang-tags or packaging.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "agree to the addition of" to "agree to consider the addition of" introduces ambiguity, as it suggests no firm commitment. The use of "may be defined" instead of "shall be defined" for definitions, and "might mean" instead of "shall mean" creates uncertainty about which products are included as licensed products. The usage of "applicable" makes it uncertain as to which of the Licensed Products would have the label requirement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 17500
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 17500 prohibits false or misleading advertising. By introducing ambiguity about which products are actually licensed and subject to the endorsement agreement (especially with CBD products involved), and about which products are required to have labels, the modified text could be construed as misleading if consumers are led to believe certain products are endorsed when they are not, or that labeling is not standardized. Misrepresenting the endorsement or product features could be a violation of this law. The 'applicable' usage introduces uncertainty to labeling standards.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 3. REPRESENTATIONS TO THE PUBLIC [17500 - 17943] ( Part 3 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) CHAPTER 1. Advertising [17500 - 17606] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) ARTICLE 1. False Advertising in General [17500 - 17509] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) 17500. It is unlawful for any person, firm, corporation or association, or any employee thereof with intent directly or indirectly to dispose of real or personal property or to perform services, professional or otherwise, or anything of any nature whatsoever or to induce the public to enter into any obligation relating thereto, to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated before the public in this state, or to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated from this state before the public in any state, in any newspaper or other publication, or any advertising device, or by public outcry or proclamation, or in any other manner or means whatever, including over the Internet, any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services, professional or otherwise, or concerning any circumstance or matter of fact connected with the propo
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity regarding which products are considered 'Licensed Products' and which products are subject to labeling requirements. This ambiguity directly contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 17500, which prohibits false or misleading advertising. The original text clearly defines 'Licensed Products' and their labeling requirements, while the changed text uses terms like 'may be defined' and 'might mean,' creating uncertainty. The use of 'applicable' introduces further uncertainty to labeling standards. As scraped Law Snippet 1 states: 'It is unlawful for any person, firm, corporation or association... to make or disseminate... any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services... in any manner or means whatever... any statement...' By creating ambiguity about which products are endorsed and labeled, the modified text risks misleading consumers, violating Section 17500. The modification weakens the original contract to the point of non-compliance, as it no longer provides a clear and enforceable definition of 'Licensed Products'. This ambiguity could allow for inconsistent or misleading advertising, thus triggering a legal violation of false or misleading advertising.
